question.text: Scenario: Mr. Rajat Singhania manages a portfolio of Nifty 50 stocks worth Rs. 2 crore for his client Mrs. Sunita Agarwal, a 58-year-old businesswoman. The current Nifty 50 level is 22,000. Rajat is concerned about potential market correction in the next 2 months due to global uncertainties. He wants to hedge the portfolio using derivatives. The following information is available: Nifty 50 Futures (2-month expiry): Trading at 22,180 (lot size: 25 units) Nifty 50 Put Option (strike 21,500, 2-month expiry): Premium Rs. 350 per unit (lot size: 25 units) Nifty 50 Put Option (strike 21,000, 2-month expiry): Premium Rs. 180 per unit (lot size: 25 units) Nifty 50 Call Option (strike 22,500, 2-month expiry): Premium Rs. 280 per unit (lot size: 25 units) Risk-free rate: 7% p.a. The portfolio has a beta of 1.0 with respect to Nifty 50 (i.e., it moves in line with the index). The number of futures contracts needed to fully hedge = Portfolio value / (Futures price × Lot size) = Rs. 2,00,00,000 / (22,180 × 25) = approximately 36 contracts. Question 2 of 5: If Rajat uses futures to hedge, he would sell approximately 36 Nifty futures contracts. If the Nifty falls to 20,000 at expiry, what is the approximate profit on the futures hedge?

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Profit per contract = (Sell price - Buy price at expiry) × Lot size = (22,180 - 20,000) × 25 = 2,180 × 25 = Rs. 54,500. Total profit = 36 contracts × Rs. 54,500 = Rs. 19,62,000. This approximately offsets the portfolio loss of Rs. 2 crore × (22,000 - 20,000)/22,000 = Rs. 18,18,182. **The Trap:** Rs. 1,96,200 would be the profit on just one contract (Rs. 54,500 is closer). Rs. 19,62,000 for 36 contracts is the correct aggregate. **Cross-Exam Context:** Futures hedging calculations are tested in VIII and X-A. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Futures hedging provides symmetric protection — it eliminates downside but also limits upside. If the market rises, the portfolio gains but the futures position loses, resulting in near-zero net return.
